html:
Nutun is not the net-cash BPO story in the prior draft. The real thesis is more interesting, but also riskier: it is a post-Transaction Capital cleanup vehicle whose remaining core asset is a leveraged South African credit-recovery platform plus a smaller offshore BPO business, trading at roughly 0.6x tangible book just as the book-buying cycle and cost base are turning.

The May 18, 2026 interim results reset the facts. H1 revenue and other income rose 9% to R1.61 billion, EBITDA rose 28% to R790 million, and Nutun South Africa EBITDA rose 36% to R710 million. The group still reported a core loss of R63 million because PBD/RTC amortization and net interest absorbed the operating cash generation, but the operating direction is no longer ambiguous. Collections improved, acquisitions of new purchased debt books resumed, net interest is easing, and management says covenants are comfortable with about R0.8 billion of unused facilities. Current price is about 102c, or R1.02, per the May 18 delayed Sharenet quote, implying roughly R800 million equity value, about $48 million at USD/ZAR near 16.66.

The variant perception is that Nutun is still treated as the wreckage of Transaction Capital and SA Taxi. That label is stale. WeBuyCars was unbundled, Mobalyz/SA Taxi was effectively exited, non-core Nutun assets were sold, and the listed company is now two focused businesses: Nutun South Africa, which buys and services unsecured NPL portfolios and runs agency collections, and Nutun International, which provides customer-engagement BPO to clients in the UK, US, and Australia.

The upside torque is in the credit book, not in a sexy AI call-center story. H1 purchased book and right-to-collect carrying value was R4.49 billion against estimated remaining collections of R8.37 billion. The equity market is valuing the entire company at only about 10% of ERC and about 60% of my post-H1 estimate of NAV. That is optically cheap because the debt is real, but it is also fundamentally cheap if the new book purchases earn acceptable returns and the recent accelerated amortization depresses reported earnings ahead of future profit recovery.

Nutun South Africa is the fulcrum. H1 PBD collections rose 28% to R773 million, RTC revenue rose 27% to R116 million, and operating costs fell 14%. That is the setup you want in a debt buyer: more collections, fewer costs, new portfolios bought at better terms, and lower interest rates beginning to flow through. A 5% swing in annualized collections or amortization assumptions is large relative to an R800 million market cap. If the business gets from loss-making to even R150 million to R250 million of normalized earnings, the current share price is suddenly 3x to 5x forward earnings.

Nutun International is not the main reason to own the stock, but it matters. Billable seats grew 20% to 2,491 and clients grew to 36, yet EBITDA fell 15% to R80 million because rand strength and hedging/translation effects hit margins. This cuts against the old “weak rand hard-currency margin tailwind” thesis. The better angle is that seat growth proves client demand is not dead, while AI is both a risk and a cost tool. Management is already deploying AI in quality assurance, workforce management, people orchestration, call analytics, and interactive voice engagement. If that reduces payroll-to-revenue and support-headcount ratios, BPO EBITDA can recover without heroic revenue growth.

The market setup is favorable because almost nobody is sponsoring the story. Sharenet shows no analyst coverage. The stock is down heavily over one and three years, trades with a wide micro-cap liquidity discount, and sits in a sector bucket that screens like consumer lending rather than a clean business-services turnaround. Retail context is mostly absence, not euphoria. This is not a crowded “AI BPO” trade.

The hard bear case is that the accounting loss is telling the truth. If collections disappoint, amortization stays elevated, and funding remains expensive, EBITDA will keep flattering the economics while equity value leaks through interest and book runoff. The other serious risk is governance. Nutun still has an executive chair structure that the JSE has allowed until December 1, 2026 to fix under the new listing requirements. That is not fatal, but it reinforces why the stock will not get a clean multiple until governance and profitability are both visibly normal.

My base case is that FY2026 remains messy, but FY2027 shows the first clean proof of normalized profitability. New FY2025 and H1 FY2026 book purchases begin contributing, rate cuts reduce interest, AI and cost actions protect BPO margins, and the market stops valuing Nutun as failed Transaction Capital residue. I would value that at 0.9x to 1.0x forward NAV, or about 165c to 175c per share.

Bear case: 65c, down 36%. Collections weaken, amortization remains high, BPO margins stay pressured by rand strength and AI-driven demand compression, and the market values the company at 0.4x book.

Base case: 170c, up 67%. Nutun returns to visible FY2027 profitability, NAV stabilizes around 170c to 180c, and the stock rerates to roughly book value.

Bull case: 300c, up 194%. Collections compound, purchased books bought in the weak cycle earn high returns, International margins recover, and normalized earnings power reaches 30c to 35c per share. At 8x to 9x forward earnings, plus evidence that book value is growing again, 300c is achievable.
